questions.explanation: Cloud Run lets you split traffic between revisions, so adjusting the traffic percentages enables gradual rollout.

questions.text: You need to keep at least 3 instances of a Cloud Run service always running to reduce cold start latency. Which setting should you modify?
options: Set CPU allocation to always on
questions.explanation: The minimum instances setting ensures a baseline number of container instances stay warm.

questions.text: A Cloud Run service experiences sudden spikes in requests and you want it to scale automatically. Which feature enables this behavior?
options: Built-in request-based autoscaling in Cloud Run
questions.explanation: Cloud Run automatically scales container instances up and down based on incoming request volume.

questions.text: You have a machine learning training job that requires specialized accelerators for large TensorFlow models. Which accelerator would you typically attach?
questions.explanation: TPUs are optimized for TensorFlow and large-scale ML workloads, providing high throughput for training.

questions.text: A general-purpose image processing workload needs significant parallel processing and benefits from GPU acceleration but is not TensorFlow-specific. Which accelerator should you use?
questions.explanation: GPUs accelerate a wide range of parallel workloads, including non-TensorFlow image processing.

questions.text: Your organization is deploying conversational agents and needs to run them on a managed runtime that integrates with Gemini Enterprise. Which component should you use?
options: Agent Runtime on Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform
questions.explanation: Agent Runtime on Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform is purpose-built for running conversational agents integrated with Gemini.

questions.text: Data scientists in your company need managed notebook environments integrated with BigQuery and the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform. Which product best meets this need?
questions.explanation: Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform Workbench provides managed notebooks that integrate with BigQuery and Gemini-based tools.

questions.text: Developers need cloud-based IDEs with secure access to source code and integration with Google Cloud resources. Which service should you use?
questions.explanation: Cloud Workstations offer managed, secure, cloud-hosted development environments integrated with Google Cloud.

questions.text: You want to view current GKE cluster inventory, including nodes and workloads, from the console. Where should you look?
options: Kubernetes Engine -> Workloads and Nodes pages
questions.explanation: The GKE Workloads and Nodes pages show running Pods, deployments, and cluster node details in the console.

questions.text: A GKE Autopilot cluster is overprovisioning Pod resources and you want to fine-tune CPU and memory requests to reduce cost. Which configuration should you adjust?
options: Pod resource requests and limits in the Pod spec
questions.explanation: In Autopilot, Pod requests and limits drive how much capacity is provisioned, so tuning them helps control cost.

questions.text: You must deploy a new version of a Cloud Run service while keeping the previous version available in case you need to roll back. What is the recommended approach?
options: Overwrite the existing revision with the new container image
Create a new revision and use traffic splitting to direct 100% of traffic to it, keeping the old revision available
Delete the old service before deploying the new one
questions.explanation: Cloud Run creates a new revision for each deployment; you can route all traffic to the new revision while preserving the old one for rollback.

questions.text: You need to restrict who can delete objects in a Cloud Storage bucket while allowing many users to read objects. Which mechanism should you use?
options: Set a lifecycle rule that prevents deletions
Use IAM roles on the bucket to grant read access broadly and write/delete access narrowly
Use only object ACLs without IAM
questions.explanation: Bucket-level IAM lets you grant read roles widely while limiting write/delete roles to a smaller group.

questions.text: Your company wants to ensure that once an object is written to a Cloud Storage bucket, it cannot be deleted or overwritten for 7 years. Which feature should you configure?
options: Lifecycle rule to delete after 7 years
questions.explanation: A retention policy with Bucket Lock enforces a minimum retention period during which objects cannot be deleted or modified.

questions.text: You want objects in a Cloud Storage bucket to automatically move from Standard to Nearline storage after 30 days to reduce cost. What should you configure?
options: Lifecycle management policy with an age condition
questions.explanation: Lifecycle rules can change storage class based on object age, automatically transitioning older objects to Nearline.

questions.text: Objects older than 365 days in a Cloud Storage bucket should be automatically deleted. Which configuration satisfies this operational requirement?
options: A Cloud Scheduler job that runs gsutil rm
A lifecycle rule with a Delete action and age condition of 365 days
questions.explanation: A lifecycle rule with a Delete action removes objects once they exceed the specified age.

questions.text: You need to query data stored in a Cloud SQL instance. Which interface can you use to run SQL queries directly in the Google Cloud Console?
options: Cloud SQL built-in query editor or client connection
questions.explanation: Cloud SQL provides a query editor in the console and connection info for client tools to run SQL queries.

questions.text: Analysts want to run ad hoc SQL queries over petabytes of data in a BigQuery dataset. Which tool in the console should they use?
questions.explanation: The BigQuery query editor is the console interface for running SQL against BigQuery datasets.

questions.text: You are operating a Bigtable cluster and need to scan time-series data with low latency. Which access pattern is recommended?
options: Use SQL queries in the BigQuery editor
Use the Bigtable HBase client libraries and design row keys for efficient scans
Export all data to Cloud Storage and read with gsutil
questions.explanation: Bigtable is accessed via HBase-compatible client libraries, and efficient row-key design is key for low-latency scans.

questions.text: Your operations team needs to estimate the monthly storage cost impact of increasing the size of a Cloud Storage bucket and adding a new BigQuery dataset. Which tool should they use?
options: Cloud Billing reports and the Pricing Calculator
questions.explanation: Billing reports and the Pricing Calculator help estimate and analyze costs for storage and BigQuery usage.

questions.text: Before a risky schema migration, you want to ensure you can restore a Cloud SQL instance quickly if something goes wrong. What should you do?
options: Create an on-demand backup of the Cloud SQL instance
Export the database to a CSV file only
Snapshot the Compute Engine VM hosting the database
questions.explanation: Creating an on-demand backup gives you a recent restore point specifically for the risky change.

questions.text: You must restore a Firestore database to a previous state after accidental data deletion. Which approach is recommended?
options: Use Firestore-managed backups and restore from a point-in-time backup if enabled
Use a VM snapshot of the Firestore server
questions.explanation: Firestore managed backups and point-in-time recovery, when enabled, provide supported restore options.

questions.text: Your team runs several long-running Dataflow jobs. You want to check whether a job is still running and see its current status. Where do you look?
options: Dataflow Jobs page in the console
questions.explanation: The Dataflow Jobs page shows job states, metrics, and logs for running and completed Dataflow jobs.

questions.text: A scheduled BigQuery query is failing. You need to review its recent execution history and error messages. Where should you go?
options: BigQuery query history and scheduled queries page
questions.explanation: BigQuery’s query history and scheduled queries UI shows past runs and their error details.

questions.text: Your organization wants a single place to monitor the health, configuration, and usage of all managed database services such as Cloud SQL, Spanner, and AlloyDB. Which tool should you use?
questions.explanation: Database Center provides a fleet-level view of managed database services, including health and configuration.

questions.text: A compliance requirement mandates that certain database tables be encrypted with customer-managed keys stored in Cloud KMS. Which feature should you configure?
questions.explanation: CMEK lets you use Cloud KMS keys you control for encrypting supported data resources like databases.

questions.text: You need to rotate the encryption keys used by a Cloud SQL instance while minimizing downtime. Which approach aligns with Google Cloud best practices?
options: Delete and recreate the instance with a new key
Use CMEK with Cloud KMS key rotation policies
questions.explanation: Using CMEK with KMS rotation policies allows key rotation with managed minimal-impact processes.

questions.text: A Cloud Storage bucket contains sensitive data and must only be accessed from approved VPC networks. Which control helps enforce this at the perimeter?
questions.explanation: VPC Service Controls define a security perimeter around services like Cloud Storage to limit access to trusted networks.

questions.text: You want to ensure that all objects in a bucket are encrypted with a specific Cloud KMS key you control. Which configuration is required?
options: Set a default CMEK key on the Cloud Storage bucket
Use signed URLs for all access
questions.explanation: Setting a default CMEK key on the bucket ensures new objects are encrypted with that key by default.

questions.text: Your BigQuery tables hold sensitive data that must be encrypted with a customer-managed key. What should you configure?
options: CMEK for the BigQuery dataset using a Cloud KMS key
questions.explanation: Configuring CMEK for the BigQuery dataset ties table encryption to a Cloud KMS key you manage.

questions.text: A Bigtable cluster is approaching storage limits. What is one operational action you can take to increase capacity?
options: Change the storage class of a Cloud Storage bucket
Add more nodes to the Bigtable cluster
Increase the size of a Cloud SQL instance
questions.explanation: Adding nodes to a Bigtable cluster increases both storage capacity and throughput.

questions.text: Your team needs to regularly review which databases are configured with backups and CMEK across the organization. Which tool is best suited for this fleet-level view?
questions.explanation: Database Center aggregates configuration details like backups and CMEK usage across managed databases.

questions.text: Your subnet 10.0.0.0/24 is running out of IP addresses. You want to expand it to 10.0.0.0/20 without downtime. What should you do?
options: Delete and recreate the subnet with a /20 range
Use gcloud compute networks subnets expand-ip-range to enlarge the subnet
Create a new subnet with a /20 range and move instances
Change the subnet mask from the VM operating system
questions.explanation: The expand-ip-range command lets you grow an existing subnet’s CIDR range without recreating it or stopping VMs.

questions.text: Which limitation applies when resizing a subnet's IPv4 range in Google Cloud?
options: Subnets can be shrunk and expanded freely
Subnets can only be shrunk, not expanded
Subnets can only be expanded, not shrunk
Subnets cannot be changed after creation
questions.explanation: IPv4 subnets are grow-only; you can enlarge the range but not make it smaller once created.

questions.text: You need a fixed external IP address for a production HTTP(S) load balancer. Which resource should you create?
questions.explanation: A static external IP is reserved and does not change, which is required for stable public endpoints.

questions.text: An internal application needs a fixed IP address within a VPC subnet to be referenced by other services. Which resource should you configure?
options: Static internal IP address in the subnet
questions.explanation: A static internal IP reserves a specific address in the subnet so other services can reliably reach it.

questions.text: You need to direct traffic from a subnet to a virtual appliance VM acting as a firewall before reaching the internet. What should you configure?
options: A Cloud NAT gateway on the subnet
A custom static route with the next hop set to the appliance VM
An additional default route to the internet
questions.explanation: A custom static route with the virtual appliance as next hop forces traffic through that VM before leaving.

questions.text: By default, how does a VPC route traffic to the internet when a VM has an external IP address?
options: Through a default Internet Gateway route
questions.explanation: VPCs include a default route to the internet gateway that handles traffic from VMs with external IPs.

questions.text: Your VMs do not have external IPs but must make outbound connections to the internet. Which service should you configure?
questions.explanation: Cloud NAT provides outbound internet access for internal-only VMs without assigning external IPs.

questions.text: You want to provide name resolution for internal resources in your VPC as well as public DNS names for your domain. Which service should you use?
questions.explanation: Cloud DNS supports both public zones for internet names and private zones for internal VPC name resolution.

questions.text: You need to create a private DNS zone for an internal domain and associate it with one or more VPC networks. Which type of Cloud DNS zone should you create?
questions.explanation: Private DNS zones are only resolvable from authorized VPC networks and are used for internal domains.

questions.text: Developers have accidentally allowed SSH from 0.0.0.0/0 to all instances. You want to quickly block this while preserving other rules. What is the best operational fix?
options: Create a higher-priority deny ingress rule for tcp:22 from 0.0.0.0/0
Change the subnet mask to limit access
questions.explanation: A higher-priority deny rule for SSH from anywhere overrides the broad allow rule without affecting other traffic.

questions.text: You want to log and analyze allowed and denied connections for troubleshooting and security. Which feature should you enable on your VPC firewall rules?
questions.explanation: Firewall rule logging records per-rule decisions on traffic, showing which connections were allowed or denied.

questions.text: Your security policy requires more advanced firewall capabilities such as application-level inspection and central policy management. Which service should you consider?
questions.explanation: Cloud NGFW adds advanced features like L7 inspection and centralized policies beyond basic VPC firewall rules.

questions.text: In a Cloud NGFW policy, you want to apply rules to workloads based on their security posture rather than IP addresses or network tags. Which construct helps you achieve this?
questions.explanation: Secure Tags classify workloads so policies can target them logically without depending on IP addresses.

questions.text: You are troubleshooting why a VM cannot reach an external IP address. Which tool can show you whether the traffic is being allowed or denied by firewall rules?
options: Connectivity Tests in Network Intelligence Center
questions.explanation: Connectivity Tests simulate traffic and show which routes and firewall rules affect a given path.

questions.text: A VM in your VPC can reach internal resources but not the internet, and it has no external IP. Cloud NAT is not configured. What is the most likely cause?
options: Missing custom static route to the internet
Missing NAT configuration for instances without external IPs
Subnets cannot access the internet at all
questions.explanation: Without external IPs, outbound internet access requires Cloud NAT; without it, only internal traffic works.

questions.text: You need to reserve a range of IP addresses on a subnet for use by container Pods on a GKE cluster. Which feature should you use?
questions.explanation: Alias IP ranges allocate secondary ranges on a subnet that GKE can use for Pod IPs.

questions.text: Your organization wants to centralize logging of network activity at the VPC edge. Which feature provides flow-level information about network traffic in and out of subnets?
questions.explanation: VPC Flow Logs capture metadata about network flows per subnet, useful for analysis and security.

questions.text: You need a static internal IP address that can be moved between instances for high availability. Which type of address should you reserve?
questions.explanation: Static internal IP addresses can be reassigned between VMs, supporting failover scenarios.

questions.text: A VM cannot resolve internal hostnames for other VMs in the same VPC. Which configuration should you verify first?
options: Cloud DNS settings and DNS policy for the VPC
questions.explanation: Cloud DNS and the VPC’s DNS policy determine how internal hostnames are resolved inside the network.

questions.text: You suspect a custom route is sending traffic to an incorrect next hop, causing connectivity issues. Where do you review and modify custom static routes for a VPC?
questions.explanation: Custom static routes are listed and edited on the VPC network’s Routes page in the console.

questions.text: You need to be notified when CPU utilization on a group of Compute Engine instances exceeds 80% for more than 5 minutes. What should you configure?
options: An alerting policy with a metric condition in Cloud Monitoring
questions.explanation: Cloud Monitoring alerting policies on CPU metrics can trigger notifications when thresholds are breached over time.

questions.text: Your application exports a custom metric such as request_latency_ms via the Cloud Monitoring API. What must you create to alert on this metric?
options: An alerting policy based on the custom metric
questions.explanation: To get notified on a custom metric, you create an alerting policy that uses that metric as its signal.

questions.text: You want to generate metrics from specific log entries and alert when they occur frequently. Which feature should you use?
questions.explanation: Log-based metrics turn filtered log entries into numeric series that you can graph and alert on.

questions.text: Your security team requires that Admin Activity audit logs and Data Access logs be retained long term and exported to BigQuery for analysis. What should you configure?
options: A log sink (log router) with a BigQuery destination
questions.explanation: A log sink routes selected logs from Cloud Logging to BigQuery for long-term storage and querying.

questions.text: You need to retain firewall and VPC Flow Logs in a different region and separate project for compliance. Which components should you configure?
options: A new log bucket with region scope and a log router to route logs to it
questions.explanation: Regional log buckets plus log routing let you store logs in a specific region and even another project.

questions.text: An application is experiencing intermittent latency spikes. You want to understand where time is spent across microservices. Which tool should you use?
questions.explanation: Cloud Trace collects distributed traces that show latency breakdowns across service calls.

questions.text: CPU usage on a Compute Engine instance is consistently high, and you suspect inefficient code. Which tool helps you identify hot functions and optimize performance?
questions.explanation: Cloud Profiler samples application performance to highlight the functions consuming the most CPU or memory.

questions.text: You need to troubleshoot a specific request that failed and want to correlate logs and traces for that request. Which two products are most relevant?
questions.explanation: Cloud Logging and Cloud Trace can share trace IDs, letting you tie detailed logs to specific traces.

questions.text: You want a single place to view service health, incidents, and how they may impact your workloads in your projects. Which dashboard should you use?
questions.explanation: The Personalized Service Health dashboard surfaces ongoing Google Cloud incidents and their impact on your projects.

questions.text: You need to ingest metrics from Prometheus instrumentation in your GKE workloads without managing your own Prometheus infrastructure. Which service should you use?
options: Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus
questions.explanation: Google Cloud Managed Service for Prometheus collects Prometheus metrics and integrates them with Cloud Monitoring.

questions.text: You want to collect both logs and system/application metrics from Compute Engine VMs using a single agent. Which agent should you deploy?
questions.explanation: The Ops Agent is the unified agent that handles both logging and metrics collection on VMs.

questions.text: Your team wants AI assistance to analyze Monitoring and Logging data and suggest optimizations or fixes for misconfigurations. Which service can help?
options: Gemini Cloud Assist for Cloud Monitoring
questions.explanation: Gemini Cloud Assist can review your Monitoring and Logging setup and recommend improvements or fixes.

questions.text: You want recommendations for rightsizing resources and cleaning up idle ones to reduce cost. Which service provides proactive suggestions?
questions.explanation: Active Assist’s Recommender component suggests ways to rightsize or remove underused resources.

questions.text: You are building a centralized view of events, alerts, and application health across multiple projects. Which product should you use?
questions.explanation: Cloud Hub aggregates operational signals like events and alerts across projects into a single view.

questions.text: You need to filter logs for a specific resource in real time and inspect the full JSON payload of a log entry. Which interface should you use?
questions.explanation: Logs Explorer is the interactive UI for filtering, viewing, and inspecting detailed log entries.

questions.text: You want to export selected logs from Cloud Logging to an on-premises SIEM system. What should you configure first in Google Cloud?
options: A log sink (log router) with a Pub/Sub or Cloud Storage destination that your SIEM can ingest
questions.explanation: A log sink routes logs to Pub/Sub or Cloud Storage, from which your SIEM can pull or receive them.

questions.text: Your application logs contain a custom JSON field user_id and you want to search by user_id in Cloud Logging. What must be configured?
options: Nothing; all fields are automatically indexed
A log schema and indexes via log analytics (if applicable)
questions.explanation: Configuring log analytics and schema/indexing ensures custom JSON fields like user_id are queryable efficiently.

questions.text: You have a complex SQL workload in Cloud SQL and want to identify slow queries and get optimization suggestions. Which tool is appropriate?
questions.explanation: Query Insights and index recommendations help analyze slow queries and suggest indexing strategies.

questions.text: You want to create a synthetic test that regularly checks the availability of an HTTPS endpoint and alerts you when it fails. What should you configure?
options: An uptime check and an alerting policy in Cloud Monitoring
questions.explanation: Uptime checks probe endpoints from various locations and can drive alerting policies when checks fail.

questions.text: To reduce noise, you want to centralize log storage for many projects into a single logging project and then apply analytics. Which feature helps route logs from multiple projects to dedicated log buckets?
questions.explanation: Aggregated log sinks route logs from multiple projects or folders into shared log buckets for centralized analysis.

questions.text: You want to see which IAM roles a particular user has on a project. Where in the console should you look?
options: IAM & Admin -> IAM page for the project
questions.explanation: The project-level IAM page lists all members and their roles, so you can see which roles a user has on that project.

questions.text: An IAM policy binding consists of which three main elements?
questions.explanation: Each binding connects a member to a role on a given resource, defining who can do what on that resource.

questions.text: You need to grant a user read-only access to all resources in a project. Which basic role should you assign?
questions.explanation: roles/viewer is the basic read-only role that lets users see resources without modifying them.

questions.text: Your security team wants to follow the principle of least privilege instead of granting broad basic roles. What type of roles should you prefer?
options: Predefined roles and custom roles scoped to specific services
questions.explanation: Predefined and custom roles are narrower and service-specific, so they better support least privilege than broad basic roles.

questions.text: Which statement best describes IAM policy inheritance in Google Cloud?
options: Policies only apply to the resource where they are set
Policies at a child resource override and remove all ancestor policies
Policies are inherited from Organization to Folders to Projects to resources, unless explicitly overridden
questions.explanation: IAM policies flow down the resource hierarchy, so roles at higher levels apply to descendants unless changed lower down.

questions.text: You assign a user a role at the Organization level. To which resources does this access automatically apply?
options: All folders and projects under that Organization
Only projects directly attached to the Organization
Only billing accounts in the Organization
questions.explanation: Organization-level roles are inherited by all folders and projects in that organization, and by the resources in them.

questions.text: A user needs to manage Compute Engine instances but should not modify IAM policies. Which predefined role is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: roles/compute.admin grants full control over Compute Engine resources without IAM administration rights.

questions.text: Which statement correctly contrasts basic roles and predefined roles?
options: Basic roles are fine-grained; predefined roles are broad
Basic roles apply globally; predefined roles apply only within a region
Basic roles grant broad permissions across many services; predefined roles are service-specific and more granular
There is no difference; they are synonyms
questions.explanation: Basic roles span many services with broad access, while predefined roles target specific services with narrower permissions.

questions.text: You want to create a custom IAM role that includes only a small set of BigQuery permissions. How can you create this role?
options: Modify a predefined BigQuery role directly
Create a custom role using gcloud iam roles create or via the IAM console, specifying the desired permissions
Edit the project-level policy JSON by hand and add permissions
Use Cloud Logging to auto-generate roles
questions.explanation: Custom roles are defined explicitly via gcloud or the IAM console by listing the exact permissions you want.

questions.text: Where can you define a custom IAM role’s scope of use?
options: At the organization level (for all projects) or at a specific project level
questions.explanation: Custom roles can be created either organization-wide or project-scoped, controlling where they can be granted.

questions.text: You granted a user the roles/storage.objectViewer role on a bucket, but they still cannot see objects. Which is a likely cause?
options: The user needs Owner role at the project level
The bucket uses uniform bucket-level access and the role was granted at the wrong level or to the wrong member
Cloud Storage is disabled in the project
The user needs billing account viewer access
questions.explanation: With uniform bucket-level access, you must grant the role on the bucket to the correct principal; mis-scoping or wrong member prevents access.

questions.text: A developer needs to manage only GKE clusters in a project. Which predefined role is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: roles/container.admin gives administrative control over GKE clusters without broad project-wide permissions.

questions.text: You want to see which specific permissions are included in a predefined role. How can you inspect these?
options: Use gcloud iam roles describe or view the role in the IAM console
questions.explanation: gcloud iam roles describe and the IAM console both list the underlying permissions of a role.

questions.text: A project owner wants to delegate user management without granting full project control. Which predefined IAM role lets a user manage roles and bindings without being Owner?
options: roles/iam.admin (or similar IAM policy admin role)
questions.explanation: IAM admin roles let someone manage IAM bindings and roles without giving them full Owner capabilities on resources.

questions.text: You want to ensure that developers cannot grant broader permissions than what is allowed by security policy. Which type of policy can restrict which roles may be granted?
options: Organization policy constraints for allowed IAM roles
questions.explanation: Organization policy constraints can limit which IAM roles are allowed to be granted in a given scope.

questions.text: A user has been granted roles/viewer at the organization level and roles/storage.objectAdmin on a specific project. How will these roles apply?
options: The organization-level viewer role prevents the project-level role from being applied
The user will have viewer on all resources plus objectAdmin on the specific project’s bucket resources
Only the more specific project-level role is used
Only the organization-level role is used
questions.explanation: Roles are additive, so the user keeps viewer everywhere and gains objectAdmin on the specific project’s storage resources.

questions.text: Which IAM member types can appear in IAM policy bindings? Choose two.
questions.explanation: Common member types include user: and group: with email addresses, along with others like serviceAccount: and domain:.

questions.text: You want to allow anyone on the internet to read objects in a specific Cloud Storage bucket. Which IAM member type might you use (though generally not recommended for sensitive data)?
questions.explanation: The allUsers member represents any anonymous user on the internet, effectively making the resource public.

questions.text: You need to audit which IAM permissions are granted to a user across multiple projects. Which approach can help?
options: Use Cloud Asset Inventory or IAM Recommender views to list IAM policies
questions.explanation: Cloud Asset Inventory and IAM-focused views can aggregate IAM policies to show what access a user has across projects.

questions.text: An auditor asks you to prove that no user has the Owner role at the organization level. Where should you verify this?
options: IAM & Admin -> IAM at the Organization level, filtered by roles/owner
questions.explanation: Checking the Organization-level IAM page and filtering for roles/owner shows whether any principal has that role there.

questions.text: You need a non-human identity for a Compute Engine VM to access Cloud Storage. What should you create?
questions.explanation: Service accounts represent workloads (like VMs) and are the right type of identity for programmatic access.

questions.text: Which statement best describes a Google-managed service account?
options: A service account automatically created and managed by Google for a specific service
A user account created in Cloud Identity
A manually created service account for custom workloads
questions.explanation: Google-managed service accounts are created by Google for services and handled automatically.

questions.text: You want a service account to have only the permissions it needs to access a specific Cloud Storage bucket. Which principle should guide which roles you grant?
questions.explanation: Least privilege means giving only the minimal roles needed to perform the required actions.

questions.text: A VM needs to read objects from a single Cloud Storage bucket. Which is the most appropriate role to grant the VM’s service account on that bucket?
questions.explanation: roles/storage.objectViewer allows read-only access to objects, matching the requirement without extra privileges.

questions.text: Where do you assign a service account to a Compute Engine VM?
options: In the IAM & Admin -> Service Accounts page only
In the VM’s configuration (during creation or update) under service account settings
In the VPC firewall rule configuration
questions.explanation: The VM’s configuration specifies which service account it runs as, controlling its identity for API calls.

questions.text: You want to prevent misuse of the default Compute Engine service account in production. Which two practices help achieve this?
options: Disable the default Compute Engine service account if not needed
Grant the default service account Owner on all projects
Use dedicated service accounts with least-privilege roles for each workload
Share the default service account key file with developers
questions.explanation: Disabling the default account and using dedicated, least-privilege service accounts reduce blast radius and misuse.

questions.text: A service account in Project A needs to read from a Cloud Storage bucket in Project B. What is the correct way to grant access?
options: Create a new service account in Project B and copy the key file
Grant the Project A service account an appropriate role on the bucket resource in Project B
Enable VPC Network Peering between the projects
Grant Owner role on Project B to the service account
questions.explanation: You grant the existing service account the needed bucket-level role in Project B, avoiding key sharing.

questions.text: You must allow a CI/CD pipeline to act as a particular service account for deployments without storing long-lived keys. Which feature should you use?
options: Service account key files stored in Secret Manager
Service account impersonation with short-lived access tokens
questions.explanation: Service account impersonation issues short-lived tokens, avoiding long-lived keys in pipelines.

questions.text: To enable service account impersonation, which role does the calling identity need on the target service account?
questions.explanation: Token-creator style roles allow generating tokens for a service account, enabling impersonation.

questions.text: You want to avoid long-lived JSON key files for service accounts. Which API can generate on-demand short-lived access tokens?
questions.explanation: The IAM Credentials API can mint short-lived tokens for a service account, replacing static keys.

questions.text: An application running on GKE needs to access Cloud Storage using a Google Cloud service account without embedding keys. Which approach is recommended?
options: Mount a JSON key file as a Kubernetes Secret
Use Workload Identity for GKE to map a Kubernetes service account to a Google Cloud service account
Use basic auth credentials in environment variables
Hard-code the access token in the container image
questions.explanation: Workload Identity maps Kubernetes service accounts to Google service accounts so Pods can obtain tokens without keys.

questions.text: Your security policy forbids service account keys. You still need workloads running on-premises or in another cloud to call Google Cloud APIs. Which feature supports this securely?
questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation lets external workloads exchange their identity tokens for short-lived Google tokens, no keys needed.

questions.text: External users from a partner company must access a Google Cloud project using their existing corporate identities, without creating Google accounts. Which identity feature should you use?
questions.explanation: Workforce Identity Federation lets human users authenticate with external identity providers to access Google Cloud.

questions.text: You want to list all service accounts in a project and see which roles they have. Where should you look in the console?
options: IAM & Admin -> Service Accounts, and IAM & Admin -> IAM
questions.explanation: The Service Accounts page lists the accounts, and the IAM page shows which roles are bound to them.

questions.text: A developer accidentally created a service account with excessive permissions. What is the best corrective action?
options: Leave it as is to avoid breaking things
Remove unnecessary roles from the service account and follow least privilege
Grant additional roles to other service accounts
questions.explanation: Removing unneeded roles reduces risk and aligns the service account with least privilege.

questions.text: You want to ensure a service account can only be used from specific VMs. Which control helps limit where a service account is used?
options: Attach the service account only to those VM instances and avoid sharing keys
Grant the service account Owner on the project
questions.explanation: If you avoid keys and only attach the service account to designated VMs, it cannot be used elsewhere.

questions.text: Your CI/CD system running outside Google Cloud needs to deploy resources using a Google Cloud service account identity without storing long-lived keys. Which configuration is best?
options: Create a service account key and store it in a local file
Use Workload Identity Federation to exchange external tokens for short-lived Google Cloud tokens
Use basic user credentials in environment variables
Open the firewall to all IP ranges
questions.explanation: Workload Identity Federation lets external CI/CD use its native identity provider and obtain short-lived Google tokens.

questions.text: You want to temporarily allow a support engineer to act as a specific service account for debugging, without sharing keys. Which mechanism should you use?
options: Grant them roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator on the service account and have them impersonate it
Share the service account JSON key
Grant them Owner on the project
Create a local user account on the VM
questions.explanation: Granting token-creator permissions lets the engineer impersonate the service account using short-lived credentials.

questions.text: Service account keys are still in use for a legacy workload. Which two best practices should you follow until you can migrate away from keys?
options: Store keys in a secure secrets manager
Share keys over email with the whole team
Rotate keys regularly and audit key usage
questions.explanation: Secrets managers and regular rotation plus auditing help reduce risk while keys are still required.

questions.text: You need to ensure a GKE workload can access Google Cloud APIs securely using a service account without managing keys. Which feature directly maps Kubernetes service accounts to Google Cloud service accounts?
questions.explanation: Workload Identity for GKE binds Kubernetes service accounts to Google service accounts for keyless access.
